section.data-narration: Electronic Waste (E-Waste) refers to discarded electronic devices, including computers, phones, televisions, and other electrical equipment. It is estimated that the global generation of e-waste has increased by 10% annually since 2010.
section.text: E-waste refers to discarded electronic devices.
Global e-waste generation has increased by 10% annually.

section.data-narration: According to the United Nations University (UNU), the global e-waste generation was estimated to be around 50 million metric tons in 2018. The e-waste generated by the European Union (EU) alone was estimated to be around 12 million metric tons in 2018. The Asia-Pacific region is expected to generate 64% of the world's e-waste by 2025.
section.text: Global e-waste generation was estimated to be around 50 million metric tons in 2018.
The Asia-Pacific region is expected to generate 64% of the world's e-waste by 2025.

section.data-narration: The growing demand for electronic devices, such as smartphones, laptops, and tablets, has contributed to the growth of e-waste. The global smartphone market is expected to reach 1.5 billion units by 2025. The number of internet-connected devices is expected to reach 75 billion by 2025.
section.text: Growing demand for electronic devices has contributed to e-waste growth.
Global smartphone market is expected to reach 1.5 billion units by 2025.

section.data-narration: The lack of effective e-waste management infrastructure in many countries is a major challenge in managing e-waste. According to the World Bank, only 20% of global e-waste is recycled or reused. The majority of e-waste is sent to landfills or incinerated, releasing toxic chemicals into the environment.
section.text: Limited e-waste management infrastructure is a major challenge.
Only 20% of global e-waste is recycled or reused.

section.data-narration: E-waste can have significant environmental and health impacts, including toxic chemicals, such as lead, mercury, and cadmium, contaminating soil and water.
section.text: E-waste can have significant environmental impacts.
Toxic chemicals can contaminate soil and water.
